13: David’s retrospective explanation of the Uzzah disaster — kî lōʾ dᵉrašnûhû kammišpāṭ, ‘because we did not seek him according to the ordinance’ — introduces the specific legal failure: the Ark was carried on a cart (ʿāgālāh, 13:7) rather than on poles by Levites (Num 4:15; 7:9). The verb pāraṣ appears again: pāraṣ bānû YHWH ʾelōhênû, ‘YHWH our God broke out against us.’ The Chronicler’s use of pāraṣ in all three chapters (13:11; 14:11; 15:13) is structural: the Uzzah incident (unauthorized pāraṣ) is explained by David in 15:13 using the same vocabulary, with the Baal-perazim victory (authorized pāraṣ) sandwiched between as a theological contrast. The Philistines’ gods were ‘carried away’ (nāśāʾ) and burned (14:12) — the Ark must be ‘carried’ (nāśāʾ) by the Levites (15:2, 15) on poles, not transported like cargo. The linguistic echo is deliberate: the verb nāśāʾ (to carry, lift, bear) governs both episodes, distinguishing the proper bearing of the Ark from the improper transport. The kammišpāṭ (‘according to the ordinance’) that David names as missing is the Mosaic regulation the Ark-cart circumvented — the failure was not ignorance but inattentiveness to the specific form YHWH had ordained.
1: David builds houses for himself in the city of David (bāttîm, the plural may indicate a palace complex) and “prepared a place for the ark of God, pitching a tent for it.” The Chronicler’s emphasis on David’s specific preparation of the ark’s site contrasts with the casual new-cart method of ch13. The tent (ʾōhel) anticipates the permanent structure Solomon will build; it is a provisional sanctum that takes the Mosaic tabernacle-form (tent over the ark) as its model. The preparation of a proper dwelling precedes the proper transfer.
2: David articulates the principle he had violated: “No one ought to carry the ark of God except the Levites, for the LORD has chosen them to carry the ark of the LORD and to minister before him forever.” The Chronicler adds this theological explanation (absent from Samuel) as the hinge between the failure of ch13 and the success of ch15. The warrant is the divine election of the Levites (bāḥar bāhem YHWH) for this specific task, making the Levitical monopoly on ark-transport a matter of covenantal appointment, not administrative preference.
3: David assembles all Israel at Jerusalem to bring up the ark — the same universal-assembly formula as ch13:5 but now properly grounded in cultic preparation. The repetition of the all-Israel assembly signals a fresh start: the previous attempt, though motivated by similar national intent, lacked the proper liturgical form. The assembly this time will be disciplined by the Levitical procedure David is about to outline.
4: David brings together the sons of Aaron and the Levites — the combined priestly and Levitical corps. The sons of Aaron (the priests) are named first, establishing the hierarchy: priests supervise, Levites carry. The subsequent verses (5–10) enumerate six Levitical subdivisions, each with its chief and a numerical contingent, giving the transfer the character of a military muster as well as a liturgical procession.
5: From the sons of Kohath: Uriel the chief with 120 kinsmen. The Kohathites were the Levitical sub-clan specifically charged with carrying the most holy objects including the ark (Num 3:30–31; 4:15). Their enumeration first in the list signals that they will take the lead role in the ark-transport, now executed correctly according to their Mosaic mandate.
6: From the sons of Merari: Asaiah the chief with 220 kinsmen. The Merarites were responsible for the structural frames, bars, and pillars of the tabernacle (Num 3:36–37); in this context they participate in the processional escort. The three major Levitical families (Kohath, Merari, Gershon) each contribute to the ark-transfer, creating a complete Levitical complement for the national procession.
7: From the sons of Gershom: Joel the chief with 130 kinsmen. The Gershonites carried the tabernacle coverings and hangings (Num 3:25–26); their inclusion in the ark-procession ensures the full Levitical unity. Joel’s appearance as a Gershomite leader is consistent with the Asaphite tradition (Asaph was a Gershonite, 6:39), and the Joel named here may be the same as Asaph’s father (cf. 6:36).
8: From the sons of Elizaphan: Shemaiah the chief with 200 kinsmen. Elizaphan was a Kohathite leader in Numbers (Num 3:30) who is given his own sub-clan registration here by the Chronicler, indicating the Levitical sub-division had grown to warrant separate administrative listing by David’s time. The 200-member contingent is the second largest after Merari, reflecting Elizaphan’s expanded clan.
9: From the sons of Hebron: Eliel the chief with 80 kinsmen. The Hebronites were a Kohathite sub-clan (Num 3:27; 26:58) associated with the town of Hebron (David’s first capital); their participation in the Jerusalem ark-procession bridges David’s Hebron and Jerusalem periods. The 80-member cohort is the smallest in this list, but the Hebronite sub-clan will play a role in later administrative assignments (26:30–32).
10: From the sons of Uzziel: Amminadab the chief with 112 kinsmen. Uzziel was another Kohathite sub-clan (Num 3:27); Amminadab as Uzzielite leader balances the Kohathite presence (Uriel, Elizaphan, Uzziel all contributing Kohathite sub-divisions). The Levitical census of six subdivisions totals 862 men for the ark-procession — a substantial escort that contrasts with the informal procession of ch13.
11: David summons the priests Zadok and Abiathar, and the six Levitical chiefs listed in vv5–10. The dual-priest formula (Zadok and Abiathar) reflects the historical situation of David’s reign where two priestly lines coexisted (Zadok was the Aaronic-Eleazar line; Abiathar was the Eli-Ithamar line). The Chronicler preserves this dual priesthood without comment; it will be resolved by Solomon’s dismissal of Abiathar (1 Kgs 2:27, 35).
12: David’s command to the priests and Levites: “Sanctify yourselves (hitqaddᵉšû), you and your fellow Levites, and bring up the ark of the LORD, the God of Israel, to the place I have prepared for it.” The Hithpael of qāḏaš (to sanctify oneself) is the standard preparatory ritual before cultic action: washing, abstention from ordinary activities, and ritual purity. The command links the success of the second ark-transport to the proper ritual preparation absent from the first attempt.
14: “So the priests and the Levites sanctified themselves to bring up the ark of the LORD, the God of Israel.” The narrative confirmation that the priests and Levites obeyed David’s sanctification command closes the preparatory sequence. The obedience to the cultic prescription matches David’s obedience to the battle prescription in ch14 (“David did as God commanded him,” 14:16), creating a parallel between military and liturgical obedience as the twin modes of Davidic faithfulness.
15: “And the Levites carried the ark of God on their shoulders with the poles on it, as Moses had commanded (kᵃʾăšer ṣiwwāh mōšeh) according to the word of the LORD.” The fulfilment formula kᵃʾăšer ṣiwwāh mōšeh is the Chronicler’s mark of proper cultic procedure; it appears throughout his narrative to signal alignment between Davidic-Solomonic action and Mosaic prescription. The poles (mōṭôt) and shoulder-carrying (bakkātēp) correspond precisely to Exod 25:14–15 and Num 7:9. The correction of ch13’s error is complete: new cart → Levitical shoulders.
16: David instructs the Levitical chiefs to appoint singers and musicians from among their fellows. The organization of the Levitical music corps is the Chronicler’s major addition to the ark-transport narrative (absent entirely from Samuel). The instruments specified — lyres (nᵉḇālîm), harps (kinnōrôt), and cymbals (mᵉṣiltāyîm) — correspond to the instruments of David’s first (failed) procession (13:8), but now properly organized under Levitical authority rather than as spontaneous popular celebration.
17: The three great Levitical musicians are appointed: Heman son of Joel (Kohathite; 6:33–38), Asaph son of Berechiah (Gershonite; 6:39–43), and Ethan son of Kushaiah (Merarite; 6:44–47). These three represent the three Levitical families and will become the founding masters of Israelite cultic music, with their descendants serving as the official singers of the first and second temples. Psalm titles bearing “Of Asaph” (Pss 73–83) and “Of Heman” (Ps 88) and “Of Ethan” (Ps 89) reflect the antiquity of this musical guilds’ tradition.
18: The second-rank Levitical singers include Zechariah and twelve others. The fourteen names of the second rank include Obed-edom the Gittite (who had hosted the ark, 13:14), now formally enrolled as a Levitical gatekeeper-singer. His inclusion resolves the ambiguity of his ethnic identity: the Chronicler places him here as a Levite, regardless of his gittî designation. The gatekeepers (šōʿărîm) had both cultic and security functions.
19: Heman, Asaph, and Ethan appointed to sound bronze cymbals (mᵉṣiltāyîm nᵉḥōšet). The three masters sound the cymbals — the percussive-signalling instrument — while their subordinates play the string instruments. The bronze cymbals (vs. the hand-held cymbals) were large instrument used for ritual signalling and processional cadence. The Chronicler’s technical precision about instrument types is characteristic of his detailed interest in Levitical musicology.
20: Eight men play lyres (nᵉḇālîm) set to Alamoth (ʿal ʿălāmôt). ʿĂlāmôt (lit. ‘maidens/young women’) is a musical term appearing also in Ps 46 (title: “according to Alamoth”), apparently indicating a higher vocal or instrumental register — possibly soprano or treble pitch. The technical musical term demonstrates that the Chronicler (or his sources) preserved genuine liturgical performance notation.
21: Six men play harps (kinnōrôt) set to the Sheminith (ʿal haššᵉmînît). Haššᵉmînît (lit. ‘the eighth’) appears in Ps 6 and Ps 12 titles and likely indicates the lower octave or bass register (an octave below Alamoth). The paired Alamoth/Sheminith directions suggest a two-register antiphonal or harmonic arrangement for the procession, matching the two groups of instruments in the Levitical orchestra.
22: Chenaniah (kᵉnanyāhû, ‘YHWH establishes’) is the Levitical master of the music (śar hallᵉwiyyîm hammassāʾ) who directed the singing (“because he was skilled,” kî ḇîn hûʾ). The word massāʾ normally means ‘burden/load’ but here has been taken to mean ‘the lifting of the voice in song’ or ‘the musical performance.’ The phrase kî ḇîn hûʾ (‘because he was discerning/expert’) uses bîn, which appears in the maśkîl psalm titles (Ps 32, 42, etc.) and refers to skilled interpretive understanding. The music director is thus characterized not just as technically proficient but as one with cultic insight.
23: Berechiah and Elkanah are gatekeepers (šōʿărîm) for the ark. The two gatekeepers flanking the ark provide security-escort alongside the musical procession. Elkanah is also a Kohathite name (6:23, 25–26) and Berechiah is Asaph’s father (6:39); these may be different individuals of the same name or the same figures appearing in multiple roles.
24: Seven priests blow trumpets (ḥăṣōṣᵉrôt) before the ark. Shebaniah, Joshaphat, Nethanel, Amasai, Zechariah, Benaiah, and Eliezer form the trumpet corps. The Mosaic prescription in Num 10:8 designates trumpet-blowing as an exclusively priestly function: “The sons of Aaron the priests shall blow the trumpets.” The Chronicler’s careful distinction between the Levitical singers/string-players and the priestly trumpet-blowers reflects this Pentateuchal hierarchy. Obed-edom and Jehiah also serve as gatekeepers, completing the full cultic personnel roster for the procession.
25: The successful ark-transport begins: “So David, the elders of Israel, and the commanders of thousands went to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD from the house of Obed-edom with joy (bᵉśimḥāh).” The combination of civil leaders (elders), military leaders (commanders of thousands), and cultic leadership (Levites) represents the full Israelite polity in unified action. The joy that marks this procession contrasts with the ambiguous celebration of ch13 that ended in death; proper order enables authentic celebration.
26: “Because God helped (ʿāzar) the Levites who were carrying the ark,” they sacrificed seven bulls and seven rams. The divine assistance formula (kî ʿāzar hāʾĕlōhîm ʾet hallᵉwiyyîm) is the Chronicler’s theological signature for the moment the holiness-danger is safely managed: God helps the Levites precisely because they are following the proper procedure. The seven-seven sacrifice is the perfect completion number for a covenant act (cf. Gen 21:28–30; 1 Kgs 18:33).
27: David’s linen robe (mᵉʿîl bûṣ, a fine-linen outer garment) and the linen ephod (ʾēpôḏ bāḏ) are his cultic vestments for the procession. The Samuel parallel (2 Sam 6:14) mentions only the linen ephod; the Chronicler adds the linen robe, aligning David more fully with the high-priestly vestments. Bûṣ (fine white linen) is the material of the temple curtains (2 Chr 3:14) and the priestly garments (2 Chr 5:12); its use for David’s procession-robe places him in the cultic register of the Levitical-priestly service. The singers and Levites are also in linen, making the procession a uniformly white-linen liturgical event.
28: “So all Israel brought up the ark of the covenant of the LORD with shouting (ûbᵉtᵉrûʿāh), with the sound of the ram’s horn (qôl šôpār), with trumpets (ḥăṣōṣᵉrôt), and with cymbals (mᵉṣiltāyîm), making music on lyres (nᵉḇālîm) and harps (kinnōrôt).” The celebratory inventory of instruments echoes 13:8 but with the proper liturgical supervision now in place. The tᵉrûʿāh (the great shout or blast) was used for both battle (Num 10:9) and cultic celebration (Num 10:10), marking this as a holy-war procession in which YHWH enters his sanctuary in triumph.
29: Michal daughter of Saul sees David leaping and dancing (mᵉraqqēḏ ûmᵉśaḥēq) and “despised him in her heart (wayyibbez lāh bibbāh).” The Chronicler reproduces this closing verse from 2 Sam 6:16 verbatim but omits the Samuel sequel (2 Sam 6:20–23) where David and Michal argue and she is declared barren. The Chronicler’s truncation avoids the domestic conflict, focusing instead on the contrast between the Saul-line (represented by Michal’s contempt) and the David-line (represented by uninhibited cultic joy). The dynastic implication is preserved — Saul’s descendants view David’s worship as undignified — without the punitive sequel.
